# Basic Data Visualization {#data-visualization}

::: {.objectives}
**Learning Objectives**

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

- Create scatterplots, line plots, bar charts, histograms, density plots, and boxplots
- Use both base R `plot()` and **ggplot2** to produce visualizations
- Map variables to aesthetics such as color, shape, and size
- Customize plot labels, themes, and legends
- Arrange multiple plots in a single figure
:::

Equipped with the knowledge of different R objects in Chapters \@ref(r-objects) and \@ref(object-other-type), and knowing how to import data sets in Chapter \@ref(import-export), we are ready to dive into the colorful world of **data visualization**. In this chapter, you will learn the most commonly used kinds of plots that can be generated in R.

> **At a glance – Chapter ROADMAP**
> 
> Section \@ref(scatterplot). **Scatterplots**: Visualize relationships between two continuous variables.  
> Section \@ref(aes-constant-value) & Section \@ref(aes-local-map). **Aesthetics**: Customize plot appearance by mapping variables to color, shape, and size.  
> Section \@ref(line-plot). **Line Plots**: Trace trends over time or ordered sequences.  
> Section \@ref(smoothline). **Smoothline Fits**: Add regression lines and confidence intervals.  
> Section \@ref(bar-charts) & Section \@ref(pie-charts). **Discrete Variables**: Create bar charts and pie charts for categorical data.  
> Section \@ref(histogram), Section \@ref(density-plot), & Section \@ref(boxplots). **Distributions**: Explore the spread of continuous data using various plot types.  
> Section \@ref(arrange-multi-plots). **Composite Figures**: Arrange multiple plots into a single layout.
